impact_profile.physical: Limitations listed include motor, walking, standing, sitting, pain, fatigue, breathing, seizures, temperature sensitivity, immune suppression, vision, hearing, speech, and body coordination issues.
impact_profile.mental_cognitive: Limitations listed include attentiveness/concentration, executive functioning, memory loss, mental confusion, information processing, learning, reading, writing/spelling, mathematics, managing time, organizing, planning, and prioritizing.
impact_profile.psychological: Limitations listed include stress intolerance, general psychological limitations, control of anger/emotions, erratic/inconsistent behavior, disruptive behavior, social skills, and inability to work alone.
impact_profile.emotional: Accommodations reduce the emotional harm of being forced to perform as though limitations do not exist. Lack of accommodation can produce shame, panic, frustration, burnout, and withdrawal.
impact_profile.social: Work-related functions include commute, off-site access, work site access, workstation access, communication, policies, safety standards, stress, temperature, and use of cognitive function, showing disability affects participation rather than only body symptoms.
impact_profile.accessibility: Accommodation categories include accessible information, accessible and usable facilities, services, technology, transportation, job restructuring, reassignment, light duty, and policies, showing that access must be systemic.

mobility_aids.definition_from_pdf: A mobility aid is a device designed to help individuals who have problems moving around complete daily activities and enjoy greater freedom and independence.

devices.name: Cane
devices.source_uses: Support for balance/stability problems, leg or trunk weakness, injury, or chronic pain; may widen base of support and reduce weight-bearing pressure on knee, hip, or leg.
source_conditions: Recovery from knee or hip surgery including joint replacement
Fragility due to older age or illness
Degenerative movement disorders such as Parkinson’s disease, multiple sclerosis, and Huntington’s disease
limitations_and_risks: Requires at least some ability to stand and coordinate steps.
Cannot overcome stairs, narrow doors, long distances, inaccessible bathrooms, broken elevators, unsafe sidewalks, or lack of seating.
May increase upper-body strain or fall risk if fitted or used incorrectly.

devices.name: Crutches
devices.source_uses: Increase base of support and transfer weight from legs to upper body for people who cannot use legs to support their weight due to short-term injury or lifelong disability.
limitations_and_risks: Requires hand, wrist, shoulder, balance, stamina, and coordination.
Underarm crutches can cause discomfort and fatigue over time.
Crutches are often unusable or unsafe in crowded, icy, wet, steep, or uneven spaces and do not solve inaccessible entrances.

devices.name: Manual Wheelchair
devices.source_uses: Manual wheelchair is for locomotion indoors and outdoors for people with mobility disability, injury, or surgery recovery; wheelchairs can enable mobility, independence, and everyday activities despite reduced mobility.
selection_factors: Measurements such as seat size and seat-to-floor height
Cushions, trays, bags, and other accessories
limitations_and_risks: Self-propulsion can require shoulder, arm, hand, trunk, and stamina capacity; transport chairs can make the user dependent on another person for propulsion.
A building can be technically close by but unusable because of steps, steep ramps, heavy doors, narrow turns, inaccessible bathrooms, cluttered aisles, gravel, snow, or curb cuts that do not line up.
Standard taxis, rideshares, and private cars may not fit the chair or may require unsafe transfers; if the wheelchair does not fit, the person may be stranded.
If a wheelchair user can transfer but the chair is left behind, the person may lose independence, safe seating, pressure relief, posture support, or the ability to leave.

devices.name: Power Wheelchair
devices.source_uses: Used by individuals with medical conditions and difficulty walking who cannot use a manual wheelchair or electric scooter; usable indoors and outdoors; can include tight-turn home models, heavy-duty outdoor models, power seating such as recline/tilt/stand, and customizations.

rear_wheel_drive: Handles small changes in gradient well
Large turning radius not ideal for small spaces
Small front wheels make curbs harder
front_wheel_drive: Larger turning radius than mid-wheel models
Can be less user-friendly for beginners
mid_wheel_drive: Center of gravity over drive wheels gives stability
Four smaller wheels increase stability and reduce tipping on uneven ground/curbs/slopes
Can feel bumps more and have less maneuverability in some situations

limitations_and_risks: Power chairs give independence only while the environment and infrastructure cooperate: accessible entrances, working elevators, charged batteries, safe curb cuts, ramp grade, door widths, turning radius, accessible transportation, and repair access all matter.

If the battery dies, joystick/controller fails, tire fails, or charger is unavailable, the chair can become an immobilized piece of heavy equipment. If the user can walk a little, they may have to get out and push or seek help; if they cannot walk or cannot safely transfer, they can be stuck in place.
Power chairs are difficult or impossible for many people to manually push due to weight, drive locks, slopes, terrain, and caregiver strain.
A standard cab or rideshare may not take the chair. A wheelchair-accessible vehicle may be unavailable, late, too expensive, or unable to fit a specific large power chair.

Emergency care can separate a person from the chair. Standard ambulances may require transfer to a stretcher and may lack a lift, ramp, tie-down, or storage for a large customized chair; leaving the chair behind can mean the person arrives at a hospital without mobility, seating, pressure relief, or a safe way home.

devices.name: Mobility Scooter
devices.source_uses: Similar to a wheelchair but configured like a motorized scooter; reduces manual strength needed to propel a manual wheelchair; useful for limited mobility, trouble walking long distances, or insufficient stamina/arm/shoulder flexibility for a manual wheelchair.
limitations_and_risks: Still requires upright posture, shoulder and hand strength, upper-body mobility, steering ability, and brake/turn control.

Scooters can fail in inaccessible environments the same way power chairs can: dead battery, no charging location, inaccessible cab, steep ramps, no elevator, tight indoor turns, snow, gravel, or broken sidewalks.
Portable scooters may be easier to transport but may sacrifice durability, seating support, range, or terrain capacity.

devices.name: Walker
devices.source_uses: Walking aid with four points of contact; provides a wider base of support than a walking stick and is used to stabilize patients with poor balance, poor mobility, or lower extremity impairment.
limitations_and_risks: Requires ability to stand, grip, lift or roll the walker, and coordinate movement.
A standard walker must be picked up to move; this can cost energy and increase fatigue.
Walkers can become unsafe on stairs, curbs, crowded buses, gravel, snow, thick carpet, narrow bathroom doors, or spaces without seating.

devices.name: Rollator
devices.source_uses: More mobile stability aid for people who can walk but need help with balance and stability; available in three- or four-wheel options; can support quicker pace and normal gait. User should be able to steer and operate handbrakes.
features: Often has wheels, brakes, seat, backrest, and storage basket/tote; heavier than walkers due to wheels and brake system.
limitations_and_risks: Requires steering, handbrake operation, judgment, and enough balance not to be pulled forward by wheels.
Can be too heavy to lift into vehicles or over curbs.
The presence of a seat helps with fatigue but does not solve inaccessible seating, entrances, bathrooms, transportation, or long wait times.

mobility_aids.key_message: Mobility aids can increase safety, comfort, freedom, and independence, but they do not make an inaccessible environment accessible by themselves.

impact_profile.physical: Aids can reduce fall risk, extend walking/rolling range, reduce weight-bearing, or replace walking; they can also create new physical demands such as gripping, pushing, transferring, charging, steering, maintaining posture, or managing pain from prolonged sitting/propulsion.
impact_profile.mental_cognitive: Choosing and using an aid adds planning: fitting, maintenance, repair, charging, transport compatibility, route access, battery range, backups, and insurance/billing.
impact_profile.psychological: A good aid can restore confidence; inaccessible reactions and public scrutiny can also create anxiety, hypervigilance, and fear of being trapped or abandoned.
impact_profile.emotional: Aids can bring relief and independence, but also grief, frustration, embarrassment from stigma, and anger when access failures blame the disabled person instead of the environment.
impact_profile.social: Aids affect work, school, dating, caregiving, social events, travel, transportation, medical care, and household participation. Social inclusion depends on accessible places and people respecting the aid as part of the person's access system.
impact_profile.accessibility: The main barrier is often not the device but the world around it: inaccessible buildings, regular cabs, broken lifts, rough terrain, no charging, no safe bathroom, no clear evacuation plan, no storage, and emergency systems that cannot transport or preserve the aid.

endocrine_disorders.definition: Endocrinology focuses on endocrine glands and hormones; hormones regulate many bodily functions, and hormone imbalance can affect the body in many ways.

conditions.name: Adrenal Insufficiency
conditions.source_description: Adrenal glands do not produce sufficient steroid hormones, primarily cortisol, which regulates sodium conservation, potassium secretion, and water retention.

conditions.name: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH)
conditions.source_description: Inherited genetic disorders affecting the adrenal glands; a person lacks one enzyme the adrenal glands use to produce hormones that regulate metabolism, the immune system, blood pressure, and other essential functions.
conditions.core_body_system: adrenal glands / inherited hormone enzyme function

conditions.name: Hyperaldosteronism
conditions.source_description: Adrenal glands make too much aldosterone, stimulating sodium absorption by the kidneys and helping regulate water and salt balance; too much aldosterone leads to hypertension and low potassium.
conditions.core_body_system: adrenal glands / kidney salt-water balance / blood pressure

conditions.name: Osteoporosis
conditions.source_description: Deterioration of bone tissue and reduction of bone strength, making bones fragile; wrists, hips, spine and other skeleton parts become vulnerable to fractures; falls can lead to serious health consequences.

conditions.name: Pituitary Disorders
conditions.source_description: Pituitary tumors are abnormal growths in the pituitary gland. Some cause too much of hormones that regulate important body functions, while others cause lower hormone levels. Most are benign adenomas that remain in the pituitary gland or surrounding tissues.

conditions.name: Thyroid Disorders
conditions.source_description: The thyroid gland produces hormones influencing metabolism and protein synthesis. Abnormal cell growth can lead to thyroid cancer; the thyroid can also be affected by hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism.

conditions.name: Hypothyroidism
conditions.source_description: Underactive thyroid gland cannot make enough thyroid hormone to keep the body running normally; common causes include autoimmune disease, surgical thyroid removal, and radiation treatment.

conditions.name: Hyperthyroidism
conditions.source_description: Overactive thyroid makes excessive thyroid hormone; body processes speed up; individuals may experience nervousness, anxiety, rapid heartbeat, hand tremors, excessive sweating, weight loss, and sleep problems.

impact_profile.physical: Hormone imbalance can affect metabolism, immune function, blood pressure, water and salt balance, potassium, bone strength, heart rate, sweating, weight, sleep, tremor, and fracture risk.
impact_profile.mental_cognitive: Endocrine instability can require symptom tracking, medication timing, lab monitoring, and crisis planning; fatigue, sleep problems, anxiety, and metabolic symptoms can affect concentration and decision-making.
impact_profile.psychological: Unpredictable body changes and chronic monitoring can cause anxiety, fear of falling/fracture, medical vigilance, or stress around symptoms being dismissed.
impact_profile.emotional: People may feel frustration, fear, or grief when body systems feel out of control or when symptoms are invisible but disabling.
impact_profile.social: Symptoms can affect work, school, exercise, appointments, sleep schedules, social plans, transportation, and daily routines.
impact_profile.accessibility: Endocrine disorders can make stairs, long walks, heat, lack of seating, inaccessible bathrooms, irregular meal breaks, rigid schedules, and delayed emergency care more dangerous.

mental_disorders.overview_from_pdf: The PDF describes an alphabetical list of mental disorders, psychological disorders, psychiatric disorders, mental illnesses, and neurological disorders gathered from multiple sources, including DSM-IV, DSM-5, ICD-10 Chapter V, online resources, and the American Psychiatric Association. It notes some older or no-longer-recognized conditions are included and local disorders may be missing.

parsed_entries.name: Absence seizure
parsed_entries.description_from_pdf: Also called the less common Petit Mal Seizure, these seizures typically cause a short period of “blanking out” or staring into space. Like other kinds of seizures, they are caused by abnormal activity in a person’s brain. [Epilepsy Foundation, Mayo Clinic]

parsed_entries.name: Abulia
parsed_entries.description_from_pdf: A pathological inability to make decisions or take action.

parsed_entries.name: Acute Stress Disorder
parsed_entries.description_from_pdf: Also known as Acute Stress Reaction and sometimes called Shock, this is a psychological condition caused by experiencing or witnessing a terrifying or traumatic event, such as a severe automobile accident, violent crime, or death. It should not be confused with the unrelated circulatory condition of shock, or the concept of shock value. Acute Stress Reaction may develop into PTSD if untreated. [PsychCentral, VA.gov]

parsed_entries.name: Adjustment Disorders
parsed_entries.description_from_pdf: Adjustment Disorder is defined as a maladaptive response to a normal, psychosocial stressor that has occurred in the past three months, and is not caused by another mental illness.

parsed_entries.name: Age-related Cognitive Decline
parsed_entries.description_from_pdf: (now known as Dementia and Neurocognitive Decline)

parsed_entries.name: Agoraphobia
parsed_entries.description_from_pdf: This is a condition where an individual doesn’t wish to go to places or face situations where they could become exposed to panic attacks.

parsed_entries.name: Akiltism
parsed_entries.description_from_pdf: [Note from MHM Staff ] We were unable to find any solid information about this disorder. Our best guess is that this disorder was once used as a catch-all for unknown diagnoses, it seems to be based on the word “akilter” meaning off-kilter or unbalanced. Our further assumption is that it was dropped as more understanding was gained and because of the extreme stigma it carried. [Merriam-Webster]

parsed_entries.name: Alcohol Addiction
parsed_entries.description_from_pdf: According to the National Council on Alcoholism and Drug Dependence, approximately 17.6 million adults abuse alcohol or are physically dependent on it.

parsed_entries.name: Amphetamine Addiction
parsed_entries.description_from_pdf: Amphetamines are some of the most addictive drugs in the world. The best way to overcome an amphetamine addiction is to get help from a professional treatment center.

parsed_entries.name: Anorexia Nervosa
parsed_entries.description_from_pdf: This is an eating disorder. The symptoms of

parsed_entries.name: Anorexia tend to be that the sufferer will eat very little, or not eat at all for a
parsed_entries.description_from_pdf: number of days, for fear of gaining weight.

parsed_entries.name: Anterograde Amnesia
parsed_entries.description_from_pdf: The loss of ability to create new memories after an amnesia event. The inability to recall recent past events, but retaining long-term memories from before the event.

parsed_entries.name: Antisocial personality disorder
parsed_entries.description_from_pdf: (also known as Sociopathy) “is A psychiatric condition in which a person manipulates, exploits or violates the rights of others … this behavior is often criminal.

parsed_entries.name: Anxiety Disorder
parsed_entries.description_from_pdf: (Also known as Generalized Anxiety Disorder) A condition in which people experience a constant state of high anxiety, which does not seem to be attributed to any particular cause. Learn more about Anxiety Treatment, Therapy and Counseling here.

parsed_entries.name: Anxiolytic-related disorders
parsed_entries.description_from_pdf: A number of different disorders that result from the abuse of a class of medications known as sedatives, hypnotics, and anxiolytics (SHA).

parsed_entries.description_from_pdf: (now part of Autism Spectrum Disorder,

parsed_entries.description_from_pdf: (Also known as ADD) Children with this condition generally have challenges paying attention to or concentrating on a given task. Find out more about testing for ADD.

impact_profile.physical: Mental and neurological conditions can include sleep disruption, appetite/nutrition changes, seizures, motor immobility or excess movement, tics, tremors, pain, panic symptoms, exhaustion, medication effects, self-injury risk, substance withdrawal, and stress-related body symptoms.
impact_profile.mental_cognitive: They can affect attention, memory, decision-making, language, learning, reality testing, impulse control, executive function, emotional regulation, body perception, sleep-wake rhythms, and ability to plan or complete tasks.
impact_profile.psychological: They can involve anxiety, depression, trauma responses, intrusive thoughts, compulsions, dissociation, delusions, hallucinations, panic, shame, unstable identity, avoidance, or distorted self-image.
impact_profile.emotional: A person may experience fear, grief, guilt, shame, anger, numbness, overwhelm, loneliness, mood swings, or relief when symptoms are understood and support is available.
impact_profile.social: Mental disorders can affect relationships, communication, employment, education, housing stability, stigma, self-advocacy, medical trust, legal/financial safety, and willingness to seek help.
impact_profile.accessibility: Mental health access is also accessibility: quiet spaces, predictable routines, flexible scheduling, remote options, sensory accommodations, trauma-informed care, plain-language instructions, crisis alternatives, transportation access, privacy, and nonjudgmental support.
